579	\section{The glued Grassmannian scheme}
580	\label{sec:gr_glued}
581	
582	\begin{definition}
583	[The Grassmannian scheme \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over \(\mathbb{Z}\)]
584	  \label{def:gr_glued_scheme}
585	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.scheme}
586	  \uses{def:gr_affine_chart, def:gr_transition, lem:gr_cocycle}
587	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
588	  % affine patches" (read from
589	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L843-L850).
590	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
591	  % "Therefore the schemes
592	  %  $U^I$, as $I$ varies over all the
593	  %  ${r\choose d}$ different
594	  %  subsets of  $\{ 1,\ldots, r\}$ of cardinality $d$,
595	  %  can be glued together by the co-cycle $(\theta_{I,J})$ to form a
596	  %  finite-type scheme $\grass(r,d)$ over $\Z$. As each $U^I$ is
597	  %  isomorphic to $\AA^{d(r-d)}_{\Z}$, it follows that
598	  %  $\grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z$ is smooth of relative dimension $d(r-d)$."
599	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
600	  Gluing the affine charts \(\{U^I\}\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}), one for each
601	  of the \(\binom{r}{d}\) subsets \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) of cardinality
602	  \(d\), along the open subschemes \(U^I_J \subseteq U^I\) via the transition
603	  isomorphisms \(\theta_{I,J} : U^I_J \xrightarrow{\sim} U^J_I\)
604	  (\cref{def:gr_transition}) -- which satisfy the cocycle condition
605	  \(\theta_{I,K} = \theta_{J,K} \circ \theta_{I,J}\) and \(\theta_{I,I} = \id\)
606	  of \cref{lem:gr_cocycle} -- yields a scheme
607	  \[
608	    \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \longrightarrow \Spec \mathbb{Z}.
609	  \]
610	  It is of finite type over \(\mathbb{Z}\) (a finite gluing of finitely
611	  generated \(\mathbb{Z}\)-algebras), and under the gluing each \(U^I\) is an
612	  open subscheme of \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) with \(U^I \cap U^J = U^I_J\).
613	
614	  Moreover \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) is \emph{smooth of relative
615	  dimension \(d(r-d)\)}. Smoothness and relative dimension are local on the
616	  source; the charts \(\{U^I\}\) cover \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\), and each
617	  \(U^I \cong \mathbb{A}^{d(r-d)}_{\mathbb{Z}}\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}) is
618	  affine space of dimension \(d(r-d)\) over \(\mathbb{Z}\), which is smooth over
619	  \(\mathbb{Z}\) of relative dimension \(d(r-d)\) (the polynomial ring
620	  \(\mathbb{Z}[x_1, \dots, x_{d(r-d)}]\) is a smooth \(\mathbb{Z}\)-algebra). As
621	  the property may be checked on an open cover of the source, the conclusion
622	  follows.
623	\end{definition}

625	\section{Separatedness}
626	\label{sec:gr_separated}
627	
628	\begin{lemma}
629	[\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) is separated over \(\mathbb{Z}\)]
630	  \label{lem:gr_separated}
631	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.isSeparated}
632	  \uses{def:gr_glued_scheme, def:gr_transition}
633	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Separatedness" (read from
634	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L856-L860).
635	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
636	  % "The intersection of the diagonal of $\grass(r,d)$ with $U^I\times U^J$
637	  %  can be seen to be the closed subscheme $\Delta_{I,J}\subset U^I\times U^J$
638	  %  defined by entries of the matrix formula $X^J_I\,X^I - X^J =0$,
639	  %  and so $\grass(r,d)$ is a separated scheme."
640	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
641	  The structure morphism \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\)
642	  (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}) is separated; equivalently, the diagonal
643	  \(\Delta : \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \times_{\mathbb{Z}}
644	  \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) is a closed immersion.
645	\end{lemma}
646	
647	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "The intersection of the diagonal of $\grass(r,d)$ with
648	% $U^I\times U^J$ can be seen to be the closed subscheme
649	% $\Delta_{I,J}\subset U^I\times U^J$ defined by entries of the matrix formula
650	% $X^J_I\,X^I - X^J =0$, and so $\grass(r,d)$ is a separated scheme."
651	% (references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L856-L860.)
652	\begin{proof}
653	  \uses{def:gr_glued_scheme, def:gr_transition}
654	  The products \(U^I \times_{\mathbb{Z}} U^J\), as \(I, J\) range over the
655	  size-\(d\) subsets, form an affine open cover of \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)
656	  \times_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\). Separatedness may be checked on this
657	  cover: it suffices to show that for each pair \((I,J)\) the preimage
658	  \(\Delta^{-1}(U^I \times_{\mathbb{Z}} U^J)\) maps by a closed immersion into
659	  \(U^I \times_{\mathbb{Z}} U^J\), i.e.\ that the corresponding ring
660	  homomorphism is surjective.
661	
662	  By \cref{def:gr_glued_scheme} the diagonal preimage is the overlap
663	  \(\Delta^{-1}(U^I \times_{\mathbb{Z}} U^J) = U^I \cap U^J = U^I_J = \Spec
664	  \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J]\). The restricted diagonal corresponds to the ring map
665	  \[
666	    \delta_{I,J} : \mathbb{Z}[X^I] \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathbb{Z}[X^J]
667	    \longrightarrow \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J],
668	    \qquad
669	    X^I \otimes 1 \mapsto X^I, \quad 1 \otimes X^J \mapsto (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I,
670	  \]
671	  the second component being the comorphism \(\theta_{I,J}\) of
672	  \cref{def:gr_transition} (a point of \(U^I_J\) and its image under
673	  \(\theta_{I,J}\) are the two coordinates of a point of the diagonal). We claim
674	  \(\delta_{I,J}\) is surjective. Its image contains all entries of \(X^I\)
675	  (from the first factor). From the second factor it contains the entries of
676	  \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\); taking the \(I\)-minor of this matrix, and using
677	  \(X^I_I = 1_{d\times d}\), the image contains the entries of \((X^I_J)^{-1}\),
678	  and in particular
679	  \[
680	    \delta_{I,J}(1 \otimes P^J_I) = \det\bigl((X^I_J)^{-1}\bigr) = 1/P^I_J .
681	  \]
682	  Hence \(1/P^I_J\) lies in the image, so the image is all of
683	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J]\) and \(\delta_{I,J}\) is surjective. Concretely the
684	  closed subscheme \(\Delta_{I,J} \subseteq U^I \times_{\mathbb{Z}} U^J\) is cut
685	  out by the entries of the matrix relation
686	  \[
687	    X^J_I \, X^I - X^J \;=\; 0,
688	  \]
689	  which on the diagonal holds because \(X^J = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\) forces
690	  \(X^J_I = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_I = (X^I_J)^{-1}\) and therefore \(X^J_I X^I =
691	  (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I = X^J\). As each restricted diagonal is a closed immersion,
692	  \(\Delta\) is a closed immersion and \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) is separated over
693	  \(\mathbb{Z}\).
694	\end{proof}

696	\section{Properness}
697	\label{sec:gr_proper}
698	
699	\begin{lemma}
700	[\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) is proper over \(\mathbb{Z}\)]
701	  \label{lem:gr_proper}

760	  Since \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) is of finite type
761	  (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}) and separated (\cref{lem:gr_separated}), and
762	  \(\mathbb{Z}\) is Noetherian, properness follows from the valuative criterion
763	  for discrete valuation rings: it suffices to show that every solid diagram
764	  \[
765	    \begin{array}{ccc}
766	      \Spec K & \xrightarrow{\ \varphi\ } & \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \\
767	      \downarrow & & \downarrow \pi \\
768	      \Spec R & \longrightarrow & \Spec \mathbb{Z}
769	    \end{array}
770	  \]
771	  with \(R\) a discrete valuation ring, \(K = \operatorname{Frac}(R)\) and
772	  valuation \(\nu : K \to \mathbb{Z} \cup \{\infty\}\), admits a unique diagonal
773	  filler \(\Spec R \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\).
774	
775	  \emph{Existence.} The morphism \(\varphi : \Spec K \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) lands
776	  in some chart \(U^I\) (\(\Spec K\) has one point), so it is given by a ring map
777	  \(f : \mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to K\). Among the \(\binom{r}{d}\) minors choose \(J\)
778	  with \(\nu(f(P^I_J))\) minimal. Because \(P^I_I = 1\) gives \(f(P^I_I) = 1\)
779	  with \(\nu = 0\), the minimum satisfies \(\nu(f(P^I_J)) \le 0\); in particular
780	  \(f(P^I_J) \ne 0\), so \(f(X^I_J) \in \mathrm{GL}_d(K)\) is invertible over
781	  \(K\). Define

784	    g(X^J) = f\bigl((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\bigr) ;
785	  \]

787	  \(f\), so \(g\) defines the same \(K\)-point \(\varphi\), now exhibited as
788	  landing in \(U^J\).

798	  \]

802	  \(P^J_{K'}\) for \(K' = (J \setminus \{j_p\}) \cup \{q\}\) -- the \(d \times d\)
803	  determinant obtained by replacing the \(p\)-th identity column of \(X^J_J\) by
804	  the column \(q\). Hence \(\nu(g(x^J_{p,q})) = \nu(g(P^J_{K'})) \ge 0\) for all
805	  entries, and the entries with \(q \in J\) are \(0\) or \(1\). Therefore
